subject: Physics
solution:
For an electromagnetic wave, the direction of wave propagation $\hat{k}$ is along the direction of $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$.

Since $\vec{E}$, $\vec{B}$, and $\vec{k}$ form a mutually perpendicular right-handed system, the direction of the magnetic field is given by $\hat{B} = \hat{k} \times \hat{E}$.

The magnitudes of the electric and magnetic fields are related by $B = \dfrac{E}{c}$.

Using the relation for the speed of light $c = \dfrac{\omega}{k}$, we get $B = \dfrac{kE}{\omega}$.

Combining the magnitude and direction gives:

$\vec{B} = B\hat{B} = \dfrac{kE}{\omega} (\hat{k} \times \hat{E})$

Since $\vec{k} = k\hat{k}$ and $\vec{E} = E\hat{E}$, this simplifies to:

$\vec{B} = \dfrac{\vec{k} \times \vec{E}}{\omega}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\vec{k} \times \vec{E}}{\omega}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Maxwell’s four equations describe the electric and magnetic fields arising from distributions of electric charges and currents, and how those fields change in time.

The equations are as follows:

∮E→·dA→=qε0   ...1∮B⇀·dA⇀=0   ...2∮E⇀·dl⇀=-∂ϕB∂t   ...3∮B⇀·dl⇀=μ0I   ...4

From the above set of equations, it is clear that only the third equation is valid for time varying field.

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using $E=E_0-e^i(k z-\omega t)$
Given, at $t=t_1, z=z_1, E=0$
the next zero that occurs in it's neighborhood is at $z_2$, the frequency of the electromagnetic wave at $t_2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&e^{i\left(k z_1-\omega t_1\right)}=e^{i\left(k z_2-\omega t_2\right)} \\
&k z_1-\omega t_1=k z_2-\omega t_2 \\
&\left(t_2-t_1\right) \omega=k\left(z-z_1\right) \\
&\text { where } k=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=2 \pi v \\
&\left(t_2-t_1\right)=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda \times 2 \pi v}\left(z_2-z_1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\quad=\frac{1}{x \times v}\left(z_2-z_1\right) \\
&\Rightarrow \lambda \times v=\frac{\left(z_2-z_1\right)}{\left(t_2-t_1\right)}=C \\
&\quad\left(t_2-t_1\right)=\frac{\left(z_2-z_1\right)}{C} \\
&\text { Frequency is } f \propto \frac{1}{t} \text { then } \frac{1}{\left(t_2-t_1\right)}=\frac{C}{\left(z_2-z_1\right)} \\
&\therefore \text { Frequency, } f=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\left(z_2-z_1\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

& =0.014 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
From the wave equation $E_y = 20\sin(3 \times 10^6 x - 4.5 \times 10^{14} t)$:
Wave number: $k = 3 \times 10^6$ m⁻¹
Angular frequency: $\omega = 4.5 \times 10^{14}$ rad/s
Speed of wave in medium: $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s
Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
Refractive index: $n = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 2$
For a nonmagnetic medium: $n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r}$
Dielectric constant: $\epsilon_r = n^2 = 4$

solution:
At every instant, the ratio of the magnitude of the electric field to the magnitude of the magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave equals the speed of light.

Therefore, EB=c

⇒EB=3×108

⇒B=E3×108=9.63×108

⇒B=3.2×10-8 T

Now, direction of magnetic field can be calculated as

B^=v^×E^

=i^×j^=k^

So, B→=3.2×10-8k^T

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \times \mu_{\mathrm{m}}}{\varepsilon_0 \times \mu_0}=\frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}^2}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}^2}} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{c}^2}{\mathrm{v}^2} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times 2=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2}{\left(1.5 \times 10^8\right)^2} \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \times 2=4 \\ & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=2\end{aligned}$
